insight: "Shock" units are defined as units that immediately threaten more damage than their tech cost upon purchase. Antima Comet does not qualify as a shock unit because it threatens 0 damage the turn it is bought.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about whether Antima Comet is a shock unit and the definition of that term.

insight: In Antima Comet races, the optimal size of your Antima scales with the rest of the set: you want the largest possible Antima, except when your opponent could get a sufficiently large one a half-turn earlier than you.
author: ruinedshadows
context: Thread discussing Antima Comet timing decisions in competitive play.

insight: In a Chrono Filter / Galvani Drone / Antima Comet set, if P1 does not buy tech on T2, P2 should immediately go for Antima and can win. The combo is so powerful that buying Antima far earlier than feels comfortable is usually correct timing.
author: zera777
context: Zera sharing post-game analysis of a Chrono/Galvani/Antima set, with community partially skeptical of the source.

insight: Countering an opponent's Antima Comet by buying your own Antima on the same turn as theirs is nearly never correct and typically loses.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy analyzing a replay where P2 mirrored P1's Antima on the same turn and lost.

insight: Arms Race counters Antima Comet when bought after it, because the opponent already has a pile of soak that becomes useful soon, and Arms Race gives them even more soak that becomes useful later, compounding the defensive advantage.
author: velizar_
context: velizar_ offering a strategic counterplay suggestion for Antima Comet.

insight: Chrono Filter is considered the primary source of degeneracy in sets containing Chrono/Galvani/Antima, not Antima itself. A proposed fix of giving alternating B and R tech each turn (instead of BR together every other turn) was discussed; mrguy888 argued it could cost 4 gold and would still be interesting while being far less degenerate.
author: mrguy888
context: Multi-person balance discussion about Chrono Filter's design problems.

insight: Buying multiple Animus units is a nerf to Wincer because multiple sources of 2 red don't mesh well with Wincer's resource needs; a single Animus (Flame Animus) works better, and you likely only want one.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 discussing resource synergy between Animus variants and Wincer.

insight: Buying two Bloodragers to deny one absorb is extremely inefficient — effectively paying 12 gold per Bloodrager, no better than Steelsplitters.
author: zera777
context: Zera posting a replay tip about Bloodrager over-purchasing to deny absorb.

insight: Mahar's optional attack mechanic means the first Mahar you buy effectively acts like a 7GG Energy Matrix, forcing the opponent to over-defend by 2; subsequent Mahars function as straightforward efficient attackers. The low tech cost for such a strong absorber makes it very powerful.
author: zera777
context: Discussion about Mahar's strategic value and whether it needs a nerf.

insight: masn6811 argued that offensive finesse (the ability to manipulate your exact damage output) does not belong in the green color pie, and has campaigned for Mahar to be changed since its release. Other experts (amalloy, jamberine) disagreed that Mahar is overpowered, noting large absorbers are intentionally strong and balanced by mutual access.
author: masn6811
context: Multi-person discussion about Mahar's design and power level.

insight: Shadowfang is cost-effective but awkward because spending 3 red each turn is difficult; if Shadowfang were 9RR instead of 7RRR, the same cost-effectiveness would make it overpowered.
author: velizar_
context: velizar_ analyzing Shadowfang's efficiency relative to its tech color requirements.

insight: Wall provides 3 soak immediately upon purchase, while Rhino provides 0 soak the turn it is bought, then 1 damage then 1 damage then 2 soak — making Wall significantly better than Rhino for pure immediate soak purposes.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy and junkmail comparing Wall vs Rhino for defensive value.

insight: Rhino's primary strategic value is not raw soak efficiency but the ability to play around threat — buying Rhino at the right moment (when it can live its full lifespan and deal its damage) creates advantages that are a major source of skill-based edges.
author: junkmail
context: junkmail arguing that Rhino is underestimated as a strategic tool beyond simple soak.

insight: junkmail proposes that units should be evaluated on three axes — specificity (how often the unit is relevant), power (how much it shifts outcomes), and set dictation (how much it shapes optimal strategy) — rather than a simple good/bad scale. Truly overpowered units score high on all three.
author: junkmail
context: junkmail presenting a framework for evaluating unit strength in Prismata.

insight: Thermite Core and Lancetooth differ fundamentally in how they use attack: Thermite Core perfectly saves any attack (always efficient), while Lancetooth is only great when you would otherwise waste attack above the absorb barrier. Buying Lancetooth when you already have 2 attack available is equivalent to buying it when you are above the absorb threshold.
author: velizar_
context: velizar_ and amalloy analyzing when to use Thermite Core versus Lancetooth in a specific game.

insight: Opening with Infusion Grid against 0 opponent attack is rarely correct because Steelsplitter can serve the same non-prompt defensive role while also threatening attack, making it a strictly more flexible choice.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy providing actionable feedback on a bot game replay review.

insight: By using a secondary absorber while Xaetron is healing, you can effectively increase your total absorb beyond what Xaetron alone would provide — a non-obvious interaction noted as advanced strategy even by experienced players.
author: zera777
context: zera777 citing 307th's blog about a Xaetron absorb optimization.

insight: Wincer should generally be held rather than fired if it cannot fire again: firing Wincer does not trade 5 drones for 15 damage outright, but rather 5 drones for 15 damage spread over 3 turns plus the option to fire again. If that future fire is impossible, the value is dramatically reduced.
author: jamberine
context: jamberine analyzing a specific Wincer game where firing was debated.

insight: Endotherm Kit should be bought early and intentionally in sets where it is correct — buying it late just because you have excess GR is wrong, because it does nothing for a very long time and the gold/green spent is largely wasted.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy explaining Endotherm Kit purchase timing in a strategic discussion.

insight: Endotherm Kit is not a bad unit — it is good in mid-to-high economy and breach-vulnerable sets that lack strong vigilant counters, and is considered less situational than Apollo among legendaries.
author: msven
context: msven defending Endotherm Kit's power level after community discussion.

insight: Animus into Cynestra is often a good alternative to Gauss Cannon: if you use the red for anything else, paying 6 gold a turn earlier than three Gauss Cannons is worthwhile. However, buying Animus solely for one or two Cynestras without using the red elsewhere is inefficient (18GGG for 3 attack with buildtime 2 is not great).
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 and amalloy discussing the value of Animus as a tech building for Cynestra.

insight: Every turn you choose not to buy a Drone, you are implicitly valuing a Drone at less than 3 gold. Therefore, selling a Drone via Ebb for 3 gold is good value as soon as you have stopped buying Drones — Ebb is not just a late-game card but becomes useful as soon as drone purchases slow down.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy and jamberine explaining why Ebb is underestimated.

insight: Shadowfang is best in high-economy sets where you can pair it with defense, or in sets with good red support. In a base+Corpus+Shadowfang set, it may be correct to skip Shadowfang entirely, with Animus-based lines potentially outperforming Shadowfang lines.
author: jamberine
context: jamberine and hyreon analyzing base+Corpus+Shadowfang set theory.

insight: Tia Threnody has an asymmetric advantage in that P2 finds it easier to obtain green tech for it due to natural Conduit, which can create first-player disadvantage in sets featuring Tia.
author: jamberine
context: Discussion about whether Tia Threnody is balanced as a legendary unit.

insight: When the opponent fires Endotherm Kit and you hold two high-HP absorbers like Chieftains against a large sword count, something is fundamentally wrong with your defensive position — likely you over-invested in lifespan defense instead of pressure.
author: sano712
context: sano712 critiquing a defensive misjudgment in a game where Endotherm Kit won.
